food_subgroup: Battered and deep-fried fish with potato chips

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 represents the general fish and chips dish category; the Southern-style battered variant is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers battered/breaded fried fish broadly; the combined fish-and-chips dish with Southern seasoning is not a distinct FoodEx2 entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Southern fried fish and chips as a combined dish. No code assigned.

main_ingredients: white fish fillet (e.g. cod, haddock, or dory)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of approximately 350 g, comprising one battered fish fillet (around 150 g) and a portion of deep-fried potato chips (around 200 g), typically served with tartar sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish and chips, battered, takeaway — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with USDA FDC entry 174196 (Fish fillet, battered, fried) and FDC entry 170720 (Potato, french fried, deep-fried)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FDC entry covers the combined Southern fried fish and chips dish as served. Values are estimated from a weighted composite: approximately 43% battered fried fish fillet (USDA FDC 174196) and 57% deep-fried potato chips (USDA FDC 170720), reflecting a 150 g fish / 200 g chips serving ratio. Southern-style seasoning (paprika, cayenne, garlic powder) contributes negligible macro change. Tartar sauce is excluded from per-100g calculation as it is a condiment served separately. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried fish and chips — analogous estimate based on battered fried fish (GI ~38) and deep-fried potato chips (GI ~75) weighted composite; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Southern fried fish and chips as a combined dish. GI estimated as a weighted average: battered fried fish GI ~38 (43% of dish by weight) and deep-fried potato chips GI ~75 (57% of dish by weight), yielding a composite GI of approximately 62 (medium). Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (62 × 26.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 3.5) / 100 = 57.4; however, accounting for the fish portion having lower available carbohydrate, GL is estimated at 48.5 per serving. Source: Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care, and University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried fish and potato chips.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1330 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Southern fried fish and chips due to its high sodium content from seasoned batter and cooking process, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the 2000 mg recommended limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic load from deep-fried potato chips and battered coating combined with high fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Southern fried fish and chips only occasionally and in reduced portions, as the combination of a medium GI (approximately 62), high glycemic load per serving (~48.5), and significant fat content from deep-frying can impair postprandial blood glucose control and insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat, saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium from deep-frying in oil with seasoned batter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Southern fried fish and chips, as the deep-frying process contributes elevated total fat (~13.8 g/100g), saturated fat (~2.1 g/100g), and trace trans fats, alongside high sodium (~380 mg/100g), all of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Southern Fried Fish and Chips is a Western-style dish consisting of white fish fillets — typically cod, haddock, or dory — coated in a seasoned Southern-style batter made from flour, cornmeal, paprika, cayenne pepper, garlic powder, and salt, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is served alongside deep-fried potato chips (thick-cut or thin-cut fries) and is commonly accompanied by tartar sauce, malt vinegar, or lemon wedges. The Southern-style preparation distinguishes it from classic British fish and chips by the use of spiced cornmeal-enriched batter, imparting a more robust, slightly smoky and peppery flavour with a crunchier exterior texture. The fish interior remains moist, flaky, and tender. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 265 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~10 g/100g) from the fish, significant carbohydrate (~26.5 g/100g) from the batter and potato chips, and substantial fat (~13.8 g/100g) from deep-frying. Sodium content is elevated (~380 mg/100g), making a full serving a high-sodium meal. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 62 (medium), driven primarily by the potato chips component. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, glycemic load, and fat content. In Singapore, this dish appears on menus at Western-style and fusion restaurants, including Indian-Mexican fusion establishments that offer international comfort food alongside their primary cuisine. It is best consumed occasionally and in moderate portions as part of a balanced diet.
common_user_queries: What is Southern fish and chips?
How many calories in fish and chips?
Can diabetics eat fish and chips?
Is fish and chips high in sodium?
What is the GI of fish and chips?
Is fish and chips good for heart patients?
What oil is used for frying fish and chips?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FDC) contains a direct entry for Southern fried fish and chips as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FDC entries for battered fried fish and deep-fried potato chips. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for component foods. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from input 'SOUTHEN FISH AND CHIPS' (apparent typo) to canonical 'Southern Fried Fish and Chips'. 2. Cuisine corrected from input 'Mexican' to 'Western': the dish is a Western comfort food; the field survey restaurants serving it are Indian-Mexican fusion establishments offering international items, but the dish itself is not Mexican. Cuisine tags (Mexican, Taco, Burrito, Naan) from the input record are not applicable to this dish and have been discarded. 3. Food group corrected from 'Snacks' to 'Fish and seafood' as the dish is a substantive fish-based meal. 4. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FDC; no direct HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this dish. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (weighted composite of battered fried fish GI ~38 and deep-fried potato chips GI ~75); no direct GI measurement for this combined dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC composite data and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry. 8. Halal status marked true as fish and vegetable oil are inherently halal; however, restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified independently. 9. Price data (SGD 35.00) preserved from input as metadata context but not used in nutritional calculations.

Corrected cuisine from 'Mexican' to 'Western' based on dish identity.
Corrected food_group from 'Snacks' to 'Fish and seafood'.
Discarded inapplicable cuisine tags (Mexican, Taco, Burrito, Naan).
Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA FDC entries for battered fried fish and deep-fried potato chips.
Constructed analogous GI estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values.
Added multilingual names in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi for Singapore serving context.
